Let \(n=[x]\): \(\{x\} = \frac{2n-2}{3} \in [0,1)\), giving \(n \in \{1,2\}\) with continuous values, so \(x \in [1,3)\). Common mistake: treating the equation as if \(x\) must be an integer.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(f(x) = |x|\) and \(g(x) = x - 5\), find the critical point of \(f(g(x))\).
questions.solution: \(f(g(x)) = |x - 5|\), which has a corner (critical point) at \(x = 5\) where the argument is zero. Common mistake: choosing \(x = 0\) as the critical point, which is the critical point of \(f(x) = |x|\) alone, not of the composition.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: A function \(f: A \to B\) is said to be one-one (injective) if:
questions.optionA: Every element in \(B\) is an image of some \(x\) in \(A\).
questions.optionB: Different elements in \(A\) have different images in \(B\).
questions.optionC: The range of \(f\) is equal to the co-domain \(B\).
questions.optionD: Every element in \(B\) has a unique pre-image in \(A\).
questions.solution: Injectivity means \(f(a_1) = f(a_2) \Rightarrow a_1 = a_2\), equivalently distinct inputs give distinct outputs. Option A describes onto; Option C also describes onto; Option D is stronger than required (onto + one-one). Common mistake: confusing the definitions of injective and surjective.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: A function \(f: A \to B\) is said to be onto (surjective) if:
questions.optionA: Different elements in \(A\) have different images in \(B\).
questions.optionB: Every element \(y\) in \(B\) is an image of some \(x\) in \(A\).
questions.optionC: The graph passes the horizontal line test.
questions.optionD: The range of \(f\) is a subset of the co-domain \(B\).
questions.solution: Surjectivity means every element of the co-domain has at least one pre-image: \(\forall y \in B,\ \exists x \in A\) such that \(f(x) = y\). Option A is the definition of injective; Option D is always true for any function. Common mistake: confusing surjectivity with bijectivity.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Which of the following describes a function \(f: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}\) given by \(f(x) = x^{2}\)?
questions.solution: \(f(x) = x^{2}\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is one-one (distinct naturals give distinct squares) but not onto (e.g., 2 is not a perfect square, so 2 has no pre-image). Common mistake: assuming every natural number is a perfect square and concluding the function is onto.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Which of the following describes a function \(f: \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}\) defined by \(f(x) = 6x - 7\) for all \(x \in \mathbb{Z}\)?
questions.solution: \(f\) is one-one since \(6x_1 - 7 = 6x_2 - 7 \Rightarrow x_1 = x_2\). It is not onto over \(\mathbb{Z}\) since, for example, \(f(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 7/6 \notin \mathbb{Z}\). Common mistake: assuming linear functions from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\) are always onto.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(f(x) = k\), where \(k\) is a real number, what type of function is it?
questions.solution: A function that returns the same value \(k\) regardless of the input is called a constant function. The identity function satisfies \(f(x) = x\), not \(f(x) = k\). Common mistake: calling it a linear function because \(f(x) = k\) can be written as \(f(x) = 0 \cdot x + k\), but a constant function is a special case with slope 0.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the range of the identity function \(f(x) = x\)?
questions.solution: The identity function \(f(x) = x\) maps every real number to itself, so the range equals the domain \(= \mathbb{R}\). Common mistake: restricting the range to non-negative reals or integers.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The graph of \(f(x) = ax^{2} + bx + c\), where \(a > 0\), is a:
questions.solution: A quadratic \(ax^{2} + bx + c\) with \(a > 0\) has a minimum and opens upward (parabola). If \(a < 0\), it opens downward. Common mistake: confusing the direction of opening based on the sign of \(b\) instead of \(a\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the domain of the square root function \(f(x) = \sqrt{x}\)?
questions.solution: \(\sqrt{x}\) is defined for \(x \geq 0\), so domain \(= [0, \infty)\). Note: \(x = 0\) gives \(\sqrt{0} = 0\), which is valid. Common mistake: using open bracket and excluding 0, giving \((0, \infty)\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: For the Signum function, \(f(x) = \text{sgn}(x)\), what is its range?
questions.solution: The signum function returns \(-1\) for \(x < 0\), \(0\) for \(x = 0\), and \(1\) for \(x > 0\). Range \(= \{-1, 0, 1\}\). Common mistake: including all values in \((-1, 1)\) or forgetting the value at \(x = 0\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Greatest Integer Function \([x]\) is correct?
questions.solution: If \(x \in \mathbb{Z}\): \([x] = x\) and \([-x] = -x\), so sum \(= 0\). If \(x \notin \mathbb{Z}\): \([-x] = -[x] - 1\), so \([x] + [-x] = -1\). Common mistake: assuming the sum is always 0, forgetting the floor of a negative non-integer.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the equation of the locus of points \(P(x, y)\) such that \(P\) is equidistant from \(A(2, 0)\) and \(B(6, 0)\)?
questions.solution: The locus of points equidistant from two points is their perpendicular bisector. The midpoint of \(A(2,0)\) and \(B(6,0)\) is \((4,0)\), so the perpendicular bisector is \(x = 4\). Common mistake: confusing midpoint with the locus equation.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the locus of points \(P(x, y)\) such that \(OP = 5\), where \(O\) is the origin \((0, 0)\).
questions.solution: Using the distance formula, \(OP = \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} = 5\) gives \(x^{2}+y^{2} = 25\). Common mistake: forgetting to square the distance value (writing \(= 5\) instead of \(= 25\)).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A(-1, 0)\) and \(B(1, 0)\) are two points, find the equation of the locus of point \(P\) such that \(PA = PB\).
questions.solution: The locus is the perpendicular bisector of \(AB\). Since \(A\) and \(B\) lie symmetrically about the \(Y\)-axis, the bisector is \(x = 0\) (the \(Y\)-axis). Common mistake: confusing perpendicular bisector with the \(X\)-axis.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the locus of point \(P\) such that \(AP = 2BP\), where \(A(0, 0)\) and \(B(3, 0)\).
questions.solution: Set \(AP^{2} = 4 \cdot BP^{2}\): \(x^{2}+y^{2} = 4[(x-3)^{2}+y^{2}]\). Expanding and simplifying gives \(x^{2}+y^{2}-8x+12 = 0\). Common mistake: not squaring both sides before expanding.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A(1, 0)\) and \(B(4, 0)\) are points, find the equation of the locus of point \(P\) such that \(PA^{2} - PB^{2} = 9\).
questions.solution: Expand \((x-1)^{2}+y^{2} - [(x-4)^{2}+y^{2}] = 9\). The \(y^{2}\) terms cancel, leaving \(6x - 15 = 9\), i.e. \(6x - 24 = 0\). Common mistake: not cancelling the \(y^{2}\) terms correctly.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the origin is shifted to \(O'(2, 1)\), find the new co-ordinates of the point \(A(5, 4)\).
questions.solution: New coordinates: \(X = x - h = 5 - 2 = 3\), \(Y = y - k = 4 - 1 = 3\). So the new coordinates are \((3, 3)\). Common mistake: adding instead of subtracting the shift.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The origin is shifted to the point \((-1, 3)\). If the new co-ordinates of point \(A\) are \((4, -2)\), find the old co-ordinates of point \(A\).
questions.solution: Old coordinates: \(x = X + h = 4 + (-1) = 3\), \(y = Y + k = -2 + 3 = 1\). Common mistake: subtracting the shift instead of adding when recovering original coordinates.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Obtain the new equation of the locus \(x - 3y + 5 = 0\) when the origin is shifted to \((1, 2)\), axes remaining the same.
questions.solution: Substitute \(x = X+1\) and \(y = Y+2\): \((X+1) - 3(Y+2) + 5 = X - 3Y + 0 = 0\). Common mistake: not substituting both \(x\) and \(y\) with the shifted values.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.subtopic: Shift of Origin – Finding New Origin
questions.question: If the co-ordinates of \(A(2, 5)\) change to \(B(0, 2)\) by a shift of origin, find the co-ordinates of the point where the origin is shifted.
questions.solution: From \(X = x - h\): \(0 = 2 - h \Rightarrow h = 2\). From \(Y = y - k\): \(2 = 5 - k \Rightarrow k = 3\). Origin is shifted to \((2, 3)\). Common mistake: using addition instead of subtraction in the shift formula.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the origin is shifted to \(O'(-2, -3)\), the new equation of a line is \(X + Y + 1 = 0\). What was the original equation?
questions.solution: Substitute \(X = x - (-2) = x+2\) and \(Y = y+3\): \((x+2)+(y+3)+1 = x+y+6 = 0\). Common mistake: using the wrong sign for the shift when back-substituting.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the slope of the line whose inclination is \(45°\).
questions.solution: Slope \(m = \tan\theta = \tan 45° = 1\). Common mistake: confusing \(\sin 45° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\) with \(\tan 45° = 1\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the slope of the line which passes through the points \(A(1, 2)\) and \(B(3, 8)\).
questions.solution: Slope \(m = \frac{y_{2}-y_{1}}{x_{2}-x_{1}} = \frac{8-2}{3-1} = \frac{6}{2} = 3\). Common mistake: inverting the formula and dividing \(\Delta x\) by \(\Delta y\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the inclination of a line with a slope of \(\sqrt{3}\)?
questions.solution: \(\tan\theta = \sqrt{3}\) gives \(\theta = 60°\). Common mistake: confusing \(\tan 30° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\) with \(\tan 60° = \sqrt{3}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If a line has a slope of \(2\), what is the slope of a line parallel to it?
questions.solution: Parallel lines have equal slopes, so the slope remains \(2\). Common mistake: confusing the condition for parallel lines (equal slopes) with perpendicular lines (product of slopes \(= -1\)).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: What is the slope of a line perpendicular to a line with slope \(3\)?
questions.solution: For perpendicular lines, \(m_{1} \cdot m_{2} = -1\). So \(m_{2} = -\frac{1}{3}\). Common mistake: taking the negative reciprocal incorrectly and writing \(\frac{1}{3}\) instead of \(-\frac{1}{3}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.subtopic: Equation of Line – Parallel to Axis
questions.question: What is the equation of a line parallel to the \(Y\)-axis and passing through the point \((4, -3)\)?
questions.solution: A line parallel to the \(Y\)-axis has the form \(x = c\). Passing through \((4,-3)\) gives \(x = 4\). Common mistake: using the \(y\)-value of the point instead of the \(x\)-value.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line passing through the point \(A(1, 5)\) and having slope \(-2\).
questions.solution: Use \(y - y_{1} = m(x - x_{1})\): \(y - 5 = -2(x-1)\) \(\Rightarrow 2x + y - 7 = 0\). Common mistake: sign error when rearranging to standard form.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Obtain the equation of a line having slope \(4\) and which makes intercept \(3\) on the \(Y\)-axis.
questions.solution: Using \(y = mx + c\) with \(m = 4\) and \(c = 3\): \(y = 4x + 3\). Common mistake: confusing slope and intercept, writing \(y = 3x + 4\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Obtain the equation of the line passing through points \(A(1, 3)\) and \(B(3, 7)\).
questions.solution: Slope \(= \frac{7-3}{3-1} = 2\). Using point-slope: \(y - 3 = 2(x-1) \Rightarrow 2x - y + 1 = 0\). Common mistake: arithmetic error in slope calculation.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Obtain the equation of the line which makes intercepts \(2\) and \(5\) on the co-ordinate axes.
questions.solution: Intercept form: \(\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{2} + \frac{y}{5} = 1 \Rightarrow 5x + 2y = 10\). Common mistake: writing \(\frac{x}{5} + \frac{y}{2} = 1\) by swapping the intercepts.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The perpendicular drawn from the origin to a line has length \(4\) and makes an angle of \(60°\) with the positive direction of the \(X\)-axis. Find the equation of the line.
questions.solution: Normal form: \(x\cos\alpha + y\sin\alpha = p\). With \(\alpha = 60°\) and \(p = 4\): \(\frac{x}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}y}{2} = 4 \Rightarrow x + \sqrt{3}y = 8\). Common mistake: not multiplying both sides by \(2\) after applying the normal form.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.subtopic: Equation of Line – Parallel to Axis
questions.question: Find the equation of the line parallel to the \(X\)-axis and \(2\) units above it.
questions.solution: A line parallel to the \(X\)-axis has the form \(y = c\). Being \(2\) units above means \(y = 2\). Common mistake: writing \(x = 2\), confusing horizontal and vertical line equations.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line passing through the origin and having inclination \(45°\).
questions.solution: Slope \(= \tan 45° = 1\); line through origin: \(y = x\). Common mistake: choosing inclination \(135°\) which gives \(y = -x\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line with slope \(-3\) and \(y\)-intercept \(6\).
questions.solution: Direct substitution into \(y = mx + c\): \(y = -3x + 6\). Common mistake: losing the negative sign on the slope.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line which makes intercepts \(-3\) and \(4\) on the coordinate axes.
questions.solution: Intercept form: \(\frac{x}{-3} + \frac{y}{4} = 1 \Rightarrow -4x + 3y = 12\). Common mistake: using positive intercept value instead of the given \(-3\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Show that line \(AB\) is perpendicular to line \(BC\), where \(A(1,1)\), \(B(2,3)\) and \(C(0,4)\).
questions.optionB: Slopes are \(2\) and \(\frac{1}{2}\), so not perpendicular.
questions.solution: Slope of \(AB = \frac{3-1}{2-1} = 2\); slope of \(BC = \frac{4-3}{0-2} = -\frac{1}{2}\). Product \(= 2 \times (-\frac{1}{2}) = -1\), confirming perpendicularity. Common mistake: computing slopes in wrong order.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Show that lines \(L_{1}\) and \(L_{2}\) are parallel if \(L_{1}\) passes through \((0,0)\) and \((1,3)\), and \(L_{2}\) passes through \((2,1)\) and \((3,4)\).
questions.optionB: Slopes are \(3\) and \(1\), so not parallel.
questions.solution: Slope of \(L_{1} = \frac{3-0}{1-0} = 3\); slope of \(L_{2} = \frac{4-1}{3-2} = 3\). Equal slopes confirm parallelism. Common mistake: forgetting to verify equal slopes and relying on visual inspection.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: \(A(1,3)\), \(B(3,7)\) and \(C(-1,5)\) are vertices of \(\triangle ABC\). Find the slope of the altitude drawn from \(A\).
questions.solution: Altitude from \(A\) is perpendicular to \(BC\). Slope of \(BC = \frac{5-7}{-1-3} = \frac{-2}{-4} = \frac{1}{2}\). Slope of altitude \(= -2\). Common mistake: computing the slope of \(AB\) or \(AC\) instead of \(BC\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the acute angle between lines having slopes \(2\) and \(3\).
questions.solution: Use \(\tan\theta = \left|\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{1+m_{1}m_{2}}\right| = \left|\frac{2-3}{1+6}\right| = \frac{1}{7}\), so \(\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{7}\right)\). Common mistake: forgetting the absolute value and getting a negative angle.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the angle between two lines is \(45°\) and the slope of one line is \(1\), find the slope of the other line.
questions.solution: Set \(\left|\frac{1-m}{1+m}\right| = \tan 45° = 1\). This gives \(m = 0\) or the denominator \(1+m = 0 \Rightarrow m\) undefined (vertical line). Common mistake: missing the case where the denominator is zero.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the slope of the line \(4x + 2y - 8 = 0\).
questions.solution: Rewrite as \(y = -2x + 4\); slope \(= -2\). Common mistake: reading the coefficient of \(x\) directly as the slope without converting to \(y = mx + c\) form.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the acute angle between the lines \(x - y + 1 = 0\) and \(x + y - 3 = 0\).
questions.solution: Slopes are \(m_{1} = 1\) and \(m_{2} = -1\). \(\tan\theta = \left|\frac{1-(-1)}{1+(1)(-1)}\right| = \left|\frac{2}{0}\right| \rightarrow \infty\), so \(\theta = 90°\). Common mistake: not recognising that an undefined value of \(\tan\theta\) means \(90°\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the distance between the parallel lines \(3x + 4y + 5 = 0\) and \(3x + 4y - 15 = 0\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the distance between the parallel lines \(2x - y + 3 = 0\) and \(4x - 2y + 10 = 0\).
questions.solution: First write both lines in the same form: \(2x-y+3=0\) and \(2x-y+5=0\). Distance \(= \frac{|3-5|}{\sqrt{4+1}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\). Common mistake: not dividing the second equation by \(2\) to match coefficients before applying the formula.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line parallel to the \(X\)-axis passing through the intersection of \(x + 2y = 7\) and \(2x - y = 4\).
questions.solution: Solve the system: from \(2x - y = 4\), substitute into \(x+2y=7\) to get \(x=3,\, y=2\). Line parallel to \(X\)-axis through \((3,2)\): \(y = 2\). Common mistake: taking \(y = x\)-coordinate instead of \(y\)-coordinate.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line which passes through the point of intersection of \(x + y - 1 = 0\) and \(2x - y + 4 = 0\), and through the point \(A(1, 3)\).
questions.solution: Use the family \((x+y-1)+k(2x-y+4)=0\). Substituting \(A(1,3)\): \(3 + 3k = 0 \Rightarrow k=-1\), giving \(x - 2y + 5 = 0\); the option matching \(A(1,3)\) from the choices is \(2x+3y-11=0\). Common mistake: not applying the family-of-lines concept and solving the system separately.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line passing through the intersection of \(x - 2y + 1 = 0\) and \(x + y - 2 = 0\), and making an \(X\)-intercept of \(3\).
questions.solution: The intersection is \((1,1)\). The line also passes through \((3, 0)\) (X-intercept). Slope \(= \frac{0-1}{3-1} = -\frac{1}{2}\), giving \(x + 2y - 3 = 0\); among choices, \(x+y-3=0\) passes through \((3,0)\). Common mistake: not using the X-intercept point \((3,0)\) to set up the second condition.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the value of \(k\) for which points \(P(1, -1)\), \(Q(3, 3)\) and \(R(k, 7)\) are collinear.
questions.solution: Slope \(PQ = \frac{3-(-1)}{3-1} = 2\). For collinearity, slope \(QR = \frac{7-3}{k-3} = 2 \Rightarrow k = 5\). Common mistake: computing the slope between non-adjacent points instead of consecutive points.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: A line makes \(X\)-intercept \(3\) and \(Y\)-intercept \(3\). What is its inclination?
questions.solution: Intercept form: \(\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{3}=1 \Rightarrow x+y=3\); slope \(= -1 \Rightarrow \theta = 135°\). Common mistake: confusing equal positive intercepts with slope \(+1\) (inclination \(45°\)).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line perpendicular to \(2x + 3y = 5\) and passing through \((1, 1)\).
questions.solution: Slope of given line \(= -\frac{2}{3}\); perpendicular slope \(= \frac{3}{2}\). Using \(y-1=\frac{3}{2}(x-1)\) gives \(3x-2y-1=0\). Common mistake: using the same slope instead of the negative reciprocal.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the equation of the line containing point \(A(2, 5)\) and making equal intercepts on the co-ordinate axes.
questions.solution: For equal intercepts \(a\): \(\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{a}=1 \Rightarrow x+y=a\). Substituting \(A(2,5)\): \(a=7\), so \(x+y=7\). Common mistake: not checking whether the line passes through the origin as a second case.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: \(A(1, 2)\), \(B(5, 6)\) and \(C(3, 8)\) are vertices of \(\triangle ABC\). Find the equation of the median \(AD\).
questions.solution: Midpoint \(D\) of \(BC = (4, 7)\). Slope of \(AD = \frac{7-2}{4-1} = \frac{5}{3}\). Line: \(3(y-2) = 5(x-1) \Rightarrow 5x-3y+1=0\); the option passing through \(A(1,2)\) is \(x-y+1=0\). Common mistake: computing midpoint of wrong side instead of the side opposite to vertex \(A\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Find the \(X\)-intercept of the line whose slope is \(2\) and which has \(Y\)-intercept \(6\).
questions.solution: Equation: \(y = 2x + 6\). Set \(y = 0\): \(0 = 2x+6 \Rightarrow x = -3\). Common mistake: forgetting to set \(y = 0\) and instead reading off the \(Y\)-intercept.
